macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Law of Gifting All Property to a Wife or Son: Apotropos vs. Real Gift

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda in the name of Shmuel: Gifting all property to a wife makes her an executor (apotropos), not a real owner
Expansion to eldest son: Same rule applies when all property is given to the eldest son
Distinction with strangers: Property given to a stranger is a real gift; property given to wife or son is treated as appointment of authority
Case of youngest son in a crib: Even if property is given to a very young infant, it is still treated as appointing an executor rather than a real gift
Disputed cases requiring resolution: Betrothed wife, divorced wife, daughter among sons, wife among brothers, stepmother
Ravina's ruling: Apotropos treatment applies to all cases except betrothed and divorced wives, which are treated as real gifts
Rav Avira's alternative: Works as a real gift (kinyan) for everything except wife among brothers and stepmother

macro_segments.title: Scope of the Apotropos Law: Does It Apply Only to Deathbed Cases or Also to Healthy Persons?

micro_segments.title: Rava's question: Does the apotropos rule apply only when a person is on their deathbed (shchiv mera) or also when healthy (bari)?
Presumption that the law applies only to shchiv mera context: A healthy person would not transfer property to ensure honor
Rashbam's analysis: Considers both immediate gifts (mehayom legamre) and gifts taking effect after death (mehayom ule'achar mita)
Question of whether healthy person would also invoke apotropos rule: Does he want the wife to have authority right away?

macro_segments.title: Case of Wife Collecting Ketubah After Property Gift: Does Acceptance of Gift Waive Lien Rights?

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Wife has lien on husband's property for her ketubah; husband gives her produce or part of property; does she waive her lien?
Answer: Giving only produce or partial property does not waive her ketubah lien on the rest
Case of gifting all property: Husband gives all property to wife; creditor comes with debt claim; wife loses both gift and ketubah rights
Rabbi Eliezer vs. Chachamim: Debate whether wife retains ketubah lien or waives it when accepting all property as gift
Rav Yehuda's anecdote: His niece (daughter-in-law) lost both gift and ketubah rights when creditor seized property
Rav Yosef bar Minyomi ruling in Rav Nachman's name: Wife must tear up her ketubah and stand on her gift; loses both

macro_segments.title: Does Rav Nachman Apply Umdana (Intent Assessment) to the Ketubah Waiver Case?

micro_segments.title: Gemara's question: Does Rav Nachman's ruling show he does NOT use umdana (assessing intent)?
Contrast case: Man gifts all property thinking son is dead; son returns alive; gift is binding (surface meaning) vs. conditional interpretation
Rabbi Shimon ben Menasya's position: Gift is not binding if giver was mistaken about crucial context (son alive); uses umdana
Rav Nachman rules like Rabbi Shimon ben Menasya in the dead-son case, showing he does use umdana for one-sided transactions
Gemara's answer: Wife would have accepted the gift even knowing there might be a creditor, unlike the man who would never gift if thinking son alive
General principle: In one-sided transactions (gifts), we assess intent (umdana); in two-sided transactions (sales), we do not

macro_segments.title: Waiving of Ketubah Rights When Property Is Distributed Among Children: Active and Passive Participation

micro_segments.title: Mishnah case: Husband distributes all property to sons and gives wife a small piece; she loses her ketubah rights
Rav's answer: Wife was an active participant (mezakeh al yadah) via kinyan, showing agreement with the distribution
Shmuel's answer: Wife need not actively participate; silence in his presence while he distributes suffices, if she receives something
Rabbi Yossi ben Rabbi Chanina's answer: Husband says to wife 'Take this land as your ketubah payment'; her silent acceptance waives rights
Gemara note: This leniency (ketubah waiver) is unique to husband-wife law; would never apply to ordinary creditors
